posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
As I approach the tail end of my 11th annual two weeks of Fall Burgundy tastings, pretty much exhausted and running on fumes, I thought that I would put out a brief shout: in moderation, harmony, purity, and classicism, 2016 has produced a stunning array of red Burgundy wines, hands down my favorite vintage alongside 2010 and 2013, with an even more approachable sensibility than 2010s sense of structure and 2013s sense of acidity.
There is a lot of detail, as always, that is needed to give a proper assessment, but I just don’t have the time and energy right now.
Yeah yeah, feel free to toss your hype hater rocks here.
Just mark my words now so that once the rest of the world gets the message, you can say, “that Panzer dude called it”.
TO THE SHARED LOVE OF THE GLORY OF BURGUNDY!!!

Well, I wrote 2 weeks ago (but I wonder how many´ve read it …):

"VERY generally … and I hadn´t the opportunity to taste as much as usual due to the early harvest in the same week …

(for red CdNuits only):
The wines are good, very good, even excellent - not as sweet nor as structured as the 2015s, they seem to be more transparent in their youth, but also due to (sometimes) lacking a bit of ripe lush mid-palate … acidity seemes to be higher than ´15 … sometimes there is a slight bitterness/dryness in the finish reg. the tannins … nothing really to worry about, but characterizing the vintage …
No really an early drinking vintage, but accessible before the 2015s … for the mid-term … and the best will certainly age for decades …
From some sites there isn´t much (if anything at all …)".

If you compare 2016 to 2013 I cannot follow you … quite different (and I´m not a huge fan of 2013).
But I agree than one can call the 2016s kind of “classic” …
however - I don´t think that the basic qualities are on the high level of 2015 … and many of the best wines will be available in extremely tiny quantities … (no good news for fans of 2016 …)

(BTW: that was my 30th vintage of tasting in Burgundy)
posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.cooked: Vintage of the Year!

posts.name: Justin Horn
posts.cooked:
Back in May I posted my impressions on 2016 reds and whites from barrel at Remoissenet. Across the board they were excellent.

During our trip to Burgundy one of the true highlights was visiting with Bernard at Domaine Remoissenet Pere et Fils. During our visit we tasted several barrel samples of his 2016 white and red Burgundies. A difficult vintage due to weather but their wines didn’t suffer the majority of the harsh realities that other wineries did. I didn’t take formal notes but let me say that their was not a mediocre wine in the bunch. They were good to excellent. For the 1st time in many years they did not make a Le Montrachet. Hail damage is to blame. Bummer.(really wanted to try this barrel sample) Here is the order of the barrel samples. All 2016.

  1. Chablis Fourchaume 1er Cru - Nice steely profile with a hint of salinity.

  2. Santenay Clos de Tavanne 1er Cru - Good depth and medium weight.

  3. Pernand Vergelesses Les Caradeux 1er Cru - Juicy and pure fun.

  4. Beaune Saint-Desire - hints of almond and various nuts. Would be perfect with smoked fish. Very unique. medium weight and slightly smoky finish. Well done.

  5. Puligny Montrachet - good citrus and minerality on the finish.

  6. Meursault Les Cras 1er Cru - Excellent Meursault. Balanced throughout with acid, nuts, smoke and citrus laden finish. Should only be enjoyed with food.

  7. Puligny Montrachet Les Folatieres 1er Cru - Medium bodied creamy throughout. Should age well. Well done.

  8. Corton Charlemagne Grand Cru - Long finish and impeccably balanced. You can taste the Grand Cru quality in this. Will try and get some of this.

  9. Beaune Les Marconnets 1er Cru - An excellent red with currants and strawberries. Well done for the vintage. Will get some of this when available.

  10. Pommard Les Rugiens 1er Cru - A new bottling for Remoissenet. I am a fan. this has good weight without sacrificing acidity. Darker fruits with a creamy syrupy finish without going too far. Should age well.

  11. Gevrey Chambertin - Dark cherry with strawberry. Lovely floral nose. A no brainer.

  12. Vosne Romanee - Well balanced with red and blue fruits. Should be a very good value.

  13. Gevrey Chambertin Poissenots 1er Cru - A small production red that every pinot lover should have. You have a mix of bright and sour fruits with a nose that makes you smile. Considering its small production should be a fair price. Will try and get when available.

  14. Gevrey Chambertin Cazetieres 1er Cru - This tastes like Grand Cru. You have earthy dark fruits with a long finish. Class in a glass. Bravo. Hope I can get some.

  15. Clos Vougeot Grand Cru - I have a soft spot in my heart for this wine. I have had a few vintages over the years and this did not disappoint. Their plot tastes like a GC. This has great depth and a richness in the finish. The nose is outstanding right from the barrel. Will age well.

  16. Charmes Chambertin Grand Cru - Well done from beginning to finish. You get earthy dark fruits with some salinity. The oldest vines in their parcels are over 75yrs old. Should age well.

posts.name: D@vid K@ntr0witz

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
While there are some lovely 2016 red Burgundies out there (many of them from producers you represent, Robert), having just returned from two months in the region and after tasting over 1,500 wines and visiting 160 domaines, I think that it’s a rather more heterogenous vintage than that.

In the Côte d’Or, the impact of frost was very varied. The frost touched some areas intensely, others not at all - even within certain climats or lieux dits, and a fortiori across communes. Frosted parcels produced grapes of 2nd and 3rd generations which ripened at a different pace. Some low-yielding frosted parcels ripened faster because of the small crop load, others seemed to suffer with blocked maturity. Add to that the impact of what some producers called the worst mildew attack in their memory: it’s hard to ripen grapes if your vines have lost all their leaves. On the other hand, of course, some parcels were not touched at all. In Morey, Clos des Lambrays and Clos du Tart produced their largest crops since 1999!

Such varied raw materials would naturally lead to above-average variation within producers’ ranges, but that variation was only amplified by the different decisions producers took in response to 2016’s unusual challenges. Consider a hypothetical grower with a parcel of Chambolle village, 50% of which was frosted to 50%, the rest of which was untouched. Half of that parcel ripened normally, the other half at a different pace - plus with grapes of 2nd and 3rd generations which ripen at a pace of their own. The grower could harvest in multiple passages (very, very expensive, especially in a year when you have much less product to sell than normal), or try to pick a compromise date: early, late, or in between. Many producers I met with reported being confronted with decisions such as this. Some frosted parcels ripened very rapidly indeed, and I tasted wines harvested in August with natural alcohols of 14%. On the other hand, producers who picked unripe 2nd and 3rd generation grapes sometimes made tart, green wines.

Then, in the cellar, consider the implications of vinfiying a massively reduced crop. Existing tanks were too small, and many producers had to use more whole cluster than usual to try to gain volume in the tanks for fermentation. After vinification, it was hard to maintain normal new oak percentages with so few barrels being filled: producers invariably used either more or less. How to preserve empty used barrels was another problem. (In this respect, choices made in 2016 will impact 2017).

Then, on the other side of the ledger, areas that were not frosted sometimes overcropped, producing dilute wines with little depth or character. In some cases, September and October rainfall amplified that, lowering both sugars and acids. So there are definitely some 2016s out there with less freshness and energy than 2015s, despite its being a cooler season overall.

I’m typing up my notes now, and there are certainly some absolutely lovely wines to be found in 2016. Cyril Audoin’s Marsannays, Emmanuel Rouget’s range, Ghislaine Barthod’s Chambolles, Thierry Glantanay’s Volnays and Pommards: happily, I could continue with a long list. And of course, sometimes lovely wines were produced despite challenging conditions, thanks to what was probably an antimony of judgement and luck, so I would certainly counsel against writing off any particular appellation on the grounds that it was frosted. But it would be a mistake to present the vintage as in any sense consistently good.

posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked:
William:

Your insights — and ability to write about them so succinctly and convincingly — are very much appreciated.

I understand the impulse to label a particular vintage as this or that. I have to admit that I do it as well sometimes … because it makes me feel like an expert and a connoisseur!

But making sweeping generalizations about such a wide swath of producers, terroir, communes just seems too reductive to me — as your excellent report illustrates.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
That’s very kind of you to say, Matthew, thanks!

Generalizations are definitely always tricky, but more so than ever with regards to the 2016 vintage. If you had just a day to taste at four domaines, it would be easy to come up with five different sets of producers which would show you five totally different versions of the 2016 vintage.

posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked: It is always impossible to generalize. But all I know is that 2016 was a pretty difficult year due to several circumstances. I am sure that the most passionate winemakers produced fine wines on their best terroirs. But these wines will be of limited quantity and pretty expensive. I am also sure that affordable 2016 wines can be had from some producers – but that is most likely not the rule but the exception because it wasn’t easy to harvest fully ripe fruit everywhere at the Cote de Nuits, Cote de Beaune and Cote Chalonnaise / Maconnaise. I recommend to taste wines first before buying anything from vintage 2016. This is especially true for the red wines.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Jürgen Steinke:

It is always impossible to generalize. But all I know is that 2016 was a pretty difficult year due to several circumstances. I am sure that the most passionate winemakers produced fine wines on their best terroirs. But these wines will be of limited quantity and pretty expensive. I am also sure that affordable 2016 wines can be had from some producers – but that is most likely not the rule but the exception because it wasn’t easy to harvest fully ripe fruit everywhere at the Cote de Nuits, Cote de Beaune and Cote Chalonnaise / Maconnaise. I recommend to taste wines first before buying anything from vintage 2016. This is especially true for the red wines.

In fact, I’d venture that it’s especially true for the white wines, but since this thread is to do with reds let’s stick to that.

Côte Chalonnaise reds were actually among the most consistent wines I tasted during my two months in Burgundy. Buy some Mercurey from Raquillet, Faiveley, Michel Juillot or Ch. de Chamirey; Rully from Dureuil Janthial or Jacquesson; Givry from Joblot and Lumpp, to single out some who did especially well. They won’t disappoint, and they won’t be expensive.

posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked: Thank you. Good to know! But the Domaines you named are amongst the most consistent these days anyway IMO.

posts.name: Fred C
posts.cooked:
I had the pleasure of tasting 2016 Dujac from barrel. And at least at Dujac, the 2016s are plain gorgeous. What struck me at the time was how pure the fruit was and how smooth the tannins were, even the Bonnes Mares. So I was a little surprised to hear Gerhard mention drying tannins in 2016. Hearing William Kelly mention the success of Morey St Denis helps explain it. Also, Dujac is Dujac.

I also bought a 2016 Frederic Esmonin Gevrey Clos Prieur out of curiosity and this thread. I was very pleasantly surprised.

  • 2016 Frederic Esmonin Gevrey-Chambertin Clos Prieur - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Gevrey-Chambertin (11/4/2017)
    Found at Whole Foods in Miami for $28 which isn’t bad if you know Miami.

Bright red cranberry fruit on the nose and palate. The fruit is surprisingly clean and pure. Maybe a hint of earth if you look for it. Tannins are very smooth and search as I may, nothing drying on the finish. A very pleasant surprise and a very pretty 2016 Gevrey.

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: James Billy
posts.cooked:
My post may have looked a bit harsh, but I too aren’t privy many facts’ and have to rely on second hand opinion. I feel uncomfortable when the writer is also peddling these wines. Maybe the writer is being 100% honest, but how would I know? William’s post came across as honest, detailed and logical. As he has no skin in the game (AFAIK), I’m much more comfortable believing him, although there are no guarantees that he is ‘right’ (please notice the inverted commas - I know it’s all opinion.) As Robert’s analysis was so different and so positive, added to the fact that it is in interests to be positive troubled me a little.

I may not use the ignore feature, but it’s nice to know it exists!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
James Billy:

My post may have looked a bit harsh, but I too aren’t privy many facts’ and have to rely on second hand opinion. I feel uncomfortable when the writer is also peddling these wines. Maybe the writer is being 100% honest, but how would I know? William’s post came across as honest, detailed and logical. As he has no skin in the game (AFAIK), I’m much more comfortable believing him, although there are no guarantees that he is ‘right’ (please notice the inverted commas - I know it’s all opinion.) As Robert’s analysis was so different and so positive, added to the fact that it is in interests to be positive troubled me a little.

I may not use the ignore feature, but it’s nice to know it exists!

Hi James,

I was attempting to qualify, not contradict. I’m assuming Robert has mainly tasted with producers he works with; and looking at wines represented on his website, I would say that if you tasted 2016s with, e.g., Olivier Lamy, Violot-Guillemard, Frédéric Lafarge, Hudelot-Noellat, Charles Audoin or Mugneret Gibourg, then you would certainly come away lyricizing about the 2016 vintage. My attempted contribution was a word of caution to the effect that not everyone did so well—including producers and cuvées from whom one normally expects excellence. I also attempted to explain some of the reasons for that heterogeneity. My view is informed by tasting and reviewing 1,500 wines at 160 or so Domaines over two months, which is not something anyone other than a professional critic is likely to have time to do. As threads like these can do a lot to establish expectations, I thought it an important qualification.

FWIW, I interpreted Robert’s initial post as an honest expression of enthusiasm for the 2016 vintage.

All best,

William

posts.name: Jeff Vaughan
posts.cooked:
I understand how Rob’s vintage comments could be taken as shill. I think it is important that with any critic, wine purveyor, or anyone posting impressions on this site, that you have to have an understanding of their palate, experience, and passion for the comments to mean anything.

I have been buying wine for years from Robert, and I have a basic understanding of his palate, and know that he is extremely passionate about what he does. He also has an understanding of my palate, and he has steered me away from buying things that he felt I might not like, without pushing another wine on me.

I know Robert sells wine to make a living, but I get the feeling from him that long term relationships are more important to him than pushing something that will get one sale, but disappoint the buyer. While his offerings are usually filled with lots of excitement, and passion, I have no doubt that Robert believes in what he writes. Whether you or I agree with him is something else.

Just my two cents.

posts.name: Bob Hughes
posts.cooked:
Agreed, Jeff - like I posted in a similar thread, we seem to have a lot of Berserkers that want to buy wines, but don’t want to have wines sold to them

Interestingly, a lot of people seem to love Terry Theise’s annual vintage catalogs for Germany, Austria and Champagne. I enjoy reading his stuff as well, but at least IMO, while well-written, Terry is also clearly in “sell mode”.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
Approaching the finish line, still in full on pant-pant-pant hustle mode, in the Mosel.
I thank everyone (haters too, i’m in a peacemaker mood) for their contributions.
As I don’t have the time for as much detail as I ideally be able to add, I would like to add these few precisions:
-As William noted, not only did Clos de Tart and Clos des Lambrays make the most wine since 1999, the entire village of Morey did.
-Gevrey as a village was mostly spared from frost.
-Santenay was completely spared from frost.
-Pommard seems to have suffered only 5-15% frost damage.
-Portions of Vosne did not suffer frost. The northern portions very much did.
I believe that these areas that had little to no frost very much illustrate the character of the untraumatized portions of the 2016 vintage, sort of what the dynamics of the 2016 cycle would bear if every producer/village got to experience peaceful frost-free abiding, even with the crazy intense mildew pressure from the June rains. At the heart of things, this is the face of 2016 that I am so wild about, and it also exists among the most diligent of the frost traumatized villages as well. As William mentioned, Cyril Audoin is a good example of this.
Things get far more complicated and require qualifiers and heterogeneous expectations once you enter trauma town o’ frost.
But as in every single vintage: producer, producer, producer.
Every single vintage presents opportunities to capture the greatness of the vintage, or fall into its trappings/shortcomings. Sometimes nature is much more generous, sometimes she is cruel.
'15s can be outstanding. They can be overripe, overly tannic, lacking energy.
'16s can be outstanding. They can be overripe, underripe, somewhat dilute.
In wildly oversimplified fashion, 1990/1991 comparison can be made with 2015/2016.
Both vintages have simply wonderful strengths that any Burgundy lover will celebrate.
It is very true that there is, especially in the frost affected parcels, way way way less wine to go around in 2016, sort of to echo what John said.
Ciao for now.

posts.name: Lee Short
posts.cooked:
Robert Panzer:

I thank everyone (haters too, i’m in a peacemaker mood) for their contributions.

Doubling down on ‘haters’ doesn’t count as peacemaking in my book. I guess we all have different standards.

posts.name: Andrew Taylor

TOP DEFINITION
shill
A person engaged in covert advertising. The shill attempts to spread buzz by personally endorsing the product in public forums with the pretense of sincerity, when in fact he is being paid for his services.
Who are you shilling for?

Ignore the n00b, he’s just here to shill.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
Nothing I have ever said is covert. It is clear as day that I am ITB. A shill is a person who pretends to not have a relationship with a guy like me, not just someone who is promoting their own interest. That is the definition of the word.
We have big boys and girls here who can make up their own minds about whether or not I have integrity, or if what I have to say has truth/merit.
But keep on tossing rocks if that is how you like to play.
The rest of us will share ideas and try to learn about early impressions of 2016 red Burgundy and the season that shaped them.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked: In all sincerity, I wonder sometimes how I can post anything positive about a region/producer that I work with and not be criticized as acting in self-interest. Simply giving the caveat that I am ITB or represent a certain producer doesn’t seem to be enough. Do please let me know what way to do so would avoid acrimony. I think that if you try to put your shoes on my feet, you would see that there isn’t a way to please everybody. It comes back to whether or not one has integrity, and that is a longer term question than one forum post, and for others to judge for themselves.

I am not so sure…

posts.name: James Billy
posts.cooked: Robert, if you don’t sell wine on commerce corner, don’t sell wine on commerce corner. Why risk looking like there’s a conflict of interest?

posts.name: Pat Burton
posts.cooked: I’ve read this thread and others that Robert has started. While I get what people are saying in terms of Robert being a retailer, I just don’t understand why most of Robert’s posts seem to be a flashpoint for some folks. He just got back from traveling through Burgundy. He comes onto the board and posts some thoughts from his trip and also posts a single offer for a single producer. What’s the big deal?

posts.name: Bob Hughes
posts.cooked:
I honestly don’t get it either, Pat. Rob sells wine for a living - he’s got it right in his signature. Not sure how anyone can be “misled” about his status. As far as I know from my conversations with him, he’s been doing this for a long time - he’s not some lawyer or investment banker who made his millions and decided to become a “wine guy” for sh*ts and giggles.

Besides the “usual suspects”, he brings in wines from places like Corsica, the Jura, Savoie, Alsace and Switzerland - if he wasn’t trying to actively market wines from “backwaters” like that, how many bottles do you think he would move?

I can tell you that his customers down here in the Philly area are uniformly positive in their assessment of what he is doing, and I personally know and drink with many of them. The wine dinners he hosts in Philly are amazing - the Burklin-Wolf dinner he did at Bibou was one of my dining highlights of 2017. He may be the only retail guy I deal with where, when he brings in something totally new to me like Dirler-Cade or Chappaz, I can just tell him to pick me out a mixed case of wine and end up being very satisfied.

But he has apparently violated the “etiquette” of this incredibly loosely-moderated Board. As some have already suggested, if his style bothers you all that much, the ignore feature is a wonderful tool.

posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked:
I buy wine from Robert.

I appreciate his enthusiasm and insights he provides from the access his business affords.

I take all his notes with a grain of salt. I like to think most Burg aficionados here are savvy enough to wade through the fluff. It’s not like we are hicks at the county fair being hustled into the tent blindly.

I do think Robert does himself a disservice by not posting more critical notes about vintages or even producers he works with. At first you might say that’s bad for business. But I’m not sure …

The glass isn’t always full or even half full in Burgundy all the time! You lose some credibility when seemingly every producer excels in every vintage. It’s just not true. I love when retailers, importers or producers are honest — like Dujac saying they underperformed in 2011.

Despite his boosterism and “homer” take on most things, I value Robert’s voice. It’s all part of the chorus here … blowhards, showoffs, cranks, reactionaries, addicts, friends, supporters, teachers, lovers

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
I have a mixed view on all of this. First, I very much value the participation of people ITB on this wine board. I am continually trying to learn about wine and value this import. If ITB people who post favorably on a wine are shouted down as shilling, they will stop participating, as many have, and we will lose a lot.

However, as Matthew says (and I will say this more broadly and not make this about any one person), ITB people who don’t post critical notes about vintages or even producers they work with quickly lose credibility and become noise even when they are correct.

So, instead of criticizing anyone, let me praise a couple of people who I think have over the years really handled this balance very well - Dan Kravitz and Martin Steinley. This is not to say that others don’t but kudos to you two.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
Appreciated dialogue.
While not a glaring example of the kind of criticism you would care to see more of, read my comments about the Belland Pommard Cras '16 that I chose not to include in my offer that I imagine you received.
Why not?
Overripe, stewy, no bueno. An example of the traumatized '16 gone bad, vs their Santenots '16 which is muy bueno, troubled gone very good.

posts.name: J0zef P1nxten
posts.cooked:
Trying to steer this thread back to its original intention, namely discussing the 2016 vintage.

I just came back from a couple of days in the Cote d’Or, arranged on very short notice as I had a free weekend. We visited only a couple of producers, namely Gouges, Amiot-Servelle, Georges Noellat and Bertheaut (unfortunately only in their shop). The weather was great, so we actually spent a lot of time just sitting on a terrace in Beaune, drinking (overpriced) coffee and strolling on the market place (see pictures).

I was positively surprised in general about the quality of the vintage, some nice wines in the barrels:

  • Gouges: very little wine available, very sad to see the empty space in the cellar… really good 2016s in my opinion, malo fermentation all finished and wines had an incredible balance and energy. Didn’t know they always destem 100%, in all vintages. Gregory Gouges is a super friendly and open person.
  • Amiot-Servelle: also hit by the frost, so did some experimenting with more whole cluster to keep sufficient volume in the tanks. They tasted quite sweet and a bit heavy to my palate, so curious whether professional critics will agree.
  • Georges Noellat: Maxime Cheurlin told they don’t export much - and then even mostly to UK, so I don’t know whether their wines are sold in the US. He is significantly expanding holdings - 27 separate cuvees made in 2016 (including white wines, which I didn’t try) - seems like he is experimenting and trying to learn about the different climats. Only tasted a couple of wines - malo not finished on a couple so difficult to taste, but overall nicely balanced!
  • Bertheaut: no 2016s to tasted - tasted a small range of 2010, 2011 and 2015, and style change between the first 2 wines and the last one is significant (more elegant, less rustic).

posts.name: D@vid R0sewater
posts.cooked:
JLee:
Robert Panzer:

In all sincerity, I wonder sometimes how I can post anything positive about a region/producer that I work with and not be criticized as acting in self-interest. Simply giving the caveat that I am ITB or represent a certain producer doesn’t seem to be enough. Do please let me know what way to do so would avoid acrimony. I think that if you try to put your shoes on my feet, you would see that there isn’t a way to please everybody. It comes back to whether or not one has integrity, and that is a longer term question than one forum post, and for others to judge for themselves.

Come on Robert, the posts were simultaneous. It’s reasonable for someone to think your post may have been marketing.

It’s great to have your input, including on producers you sell. Maybe if you included a disclaimer that you’re selling wine related to your post people would be less suspicious.

Cheers

Robert, FWIW this is where I am - I appreciate your enthusiasm and given how well your selections overlap with my palate and my satisfaction with the wines you’ve sold me that I’ve had and you’ve endorsed, I don’t think you are making things up just to sell.

However, if you want to be totally above board, “ITB” isn’t enough - best to add the disclaimer that you sell wines that are related to your post is the way to go. Actually, I think more people believe posts that fully disclose conflicts of interest than they do when someone else has to bring them up…and they inevitably will.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
Thanks David.
I will in the future try to double and triple down on disclaimers in an attempt to avoid the inevitable (is that a paradox?)
How is this for a global disclaimer: I will be selling Burgundy/German wines from '16, '17, '18 ad infinitum, until I die or retire (or both at the same time).
I wonder if I can fit that in my signature…
;0

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
Disclaimer: I represent Amiot Servelle and Georges Noellat.
Amiot has used stems for years, in varying degree based on the vintage.
I don’t think the critics will say bad things at all, is my guess. I liked the '16s quite a bit, much more than the '15s, which I found to be harder than I would like, except at the very tippy top (the Amoureuses is very yum yum in '15, Charmes Chambertin too). I pretty much said as much in my offer (to Mr King’s point about me not being critical of my own producers). The '16s had much more suavity of texture and charm, which is pretty important to me in my Chambolle. I imagine the '15s will be solid (no pun intended) longer distance runners with such structure, but not so easy to cuddle up to in youth. The '16s will be much more approachable, and possibly not age as long. They made outstanding Clos Saint Denis and Charmes in '16, part of the “Morey did very well in '16” theme. I prefer their '14s to their '15s, in most cases. Talking about the overlooked moderation and pleasure of the '14 Burgundy vintage in general is another can of worms, perhaps for another thread.

Maxime’s '16s were one of the shining top highlights of my trip. He made outrageously good wines, to my taste. Knee buckling beauty, harmony. A few gassy weirdos that didn’t want to play, but man oh man…the “kid” is a vinification savant, if there ever was such a thing.

I love many of Amelie’s wines in general, with the '13s being particularly my cup of tea. She is finding her way, just fine. I don’t represent her, and didn’t taste with her, as much as I would have liked to. Only so much time when you are crunching in appointments.
She is very sweet and genuine.

posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked:
Thanks for thoughts Robert.

As an example, Georges Noellat is a winemaker I likely would not have tried if it weren’t for Robert’s tub-thumping. And it’s much to my gain. (Maxime’s VR Chaumes is right in my wheelhouse – reddish, transparent, gently spiced and oh-so succulent). I write this not so much to defend the unstoppable force that is Panzer but to urge people to try Maxime’s wines.

posts.name: Rob Weaver
posts.cooked:
Robert has been instrumental in helping me build a very nice selection of Burgundy in my cellar, where there was once none. He sells great wine at great prices, his enthusiasm is genuine and over the top, all at the same time. What’s not to like?

I’m glad that the consumer watchdogs did not protect me from him.

Robert Panzer:

Appreciated dialogue.
While not a glaring example of the kind of criticism you would care to see more of, read my comments about the Belland Pommard Cras '16 that I chose not to include in my offer that I imagine you received.
Why not?
Overripe, stewy, no bueno. An example of the traumatized '16 gone bad, vs their Santenots '16 which is muy bueno, troubled gone very good.

Ah Belland. I must say that I was disappointed this year. I left a few years since my last visit (the 09s) when papi-Belland was already saying that ‘Julie wants less wood, and less extraction - that’s the kids for you’ I assumed that this would be the gradual direction going forward. So to come and taste the 2016s and see absolutely no change was, for my palate, such a disappointment. Belland has always produced big, chunky wines, often quite delicious in their style - and I’ve bought and enjoyed a few - but with not a lot of grace. And nothing has changed…

Fortunately in Santenay there is:
Anne-Marie and Jean-Marc Vincent
David Moreau
Antoine Olivier
The two Muzards
and more recently Jessiaume - which is at least three worthy domaines more than there was 10 years ago…

Bill,

What are your thoughts on Lequin-Collin?

Just started selling them and can find almost no critical history, curious of your opinion…

posts.name: Peter Kleban
posts.cooked:
Matthew King:

Despite his boosterism and “homer” take on most things, I value Robert’s voice. It’s all part of the chorus here … blowhards, showoffs, cranks, reactionaries, addicts, friends, supporters, teachers, lovers

A bit OT, but what a wonderfully poetic description of WB! Thank you, Matthew.

jbray23:

Bill,

What are your thoughts on Lequin-Collin?

Just started selling them and can find almost no critical history, curious of your opinion…

I get about a bit, Jason, but I honestly never met one. Last time I had a weekend wander around Santenay (just about a month ago) I did take a picture of the domaine sign as an aide-memoire to visit. But then I still forgot it
Apart from a couple of ‘stragglers’ I’ve just moved on from white to red domaines for 2016s, so haven’t any short-term plans to visit…

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
When did you taste there Bill?
A week ago when I tasted, chunky and big, without grace is nothing that ever ever could have occurred to me.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked: And we are talking about the '16s, right?

Robert Panzer:

When did you taste there Bill?
A week ago when I tasted, chunky and big, without grace is nothing that ever ever could have occurred to me.

almost 3 weeks ago…

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
I have respect for your work and palate (ladybug fanaticism aside), but am at a loss to imagine we weren’t tasting in different worlds. In precision, purity, and grace, the '16s were shockingly good on my day.
Indeed, wines have weird phases/days, like the Vigot '16s that were tasting only on shrill structure/acidity, which is not their character according to the Vigots. I will wait for them to be bottled and then taste to catch them in a more settled place.
But I really can’t imagine such Belland impressions, and it isn’t from self interested blinders.

Robert Panzer:

I have respect for your work and palate (ladybug fanaticism aside), but am at a loss to imagine we weren’t tasting in different worlds. In precision, purity, and grace, the '16s were shockingly good on my day.
Indeed, wines have weird phases/days, like the Vigot '16s that were tasting only on shrill structure/acidity, which is not their character according to the Vigots. I will wait for them to be bottled and then taste to catch them in a more settled place.
But I really can’t imagine such Belland impressions, and it isn’t from self interested blinders.

Your palate, my palate, Robert. You choose to put in a dig about bugs because you can’t or won’t see it, you also choose to focus on one word - grace - but my statement was rather balanced: “Belland has always produced big, chunky wines, often quite delicious in their style - and I’ve bought and enjoyed a few - but with not a lot of grace.”

Chiu Lin - yes Roger.
I’ve also a few 05 and 06s left. Big kudos to Roger, with those vintages he was one of the very first to release wines with DIAM5 seals - and they are still excellent today - 12 years later…

posts.name: Ian Fitzsimmons
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:
Jürgen Steinke:

It is always impossible to generalize. But all I know is that 2016 was a pretty difficult year due to several circumstances. I am sure that the most passionate winemakers produced fine wines on their best terroirs. But these wines will be of limited quantity and pretty expensive. I am also sure that affordable 2016 wines can be had from some producers – but that is most likely not the rule but the exception because it wasn’t easy to harvest fully ripe fruit everywhere at the Cote de Nuits, Cote de Beaune and Cote Chalonnaise / Maconnaise. I recommend to taste wines first before buying anything from vintage 2016. This is especially true for the red wines.

In fact, I’d venture that it’s especially true for the white wines, but since this thread is to do with reds let’s stick to that.

Côte Chalonnaise reds were actually among the most consistent wines I tasted during my two months in Burgundy. Buy some Mercurey from Raquillet, Faiveley, Michel Juillot or Ch. de Chamirey; Rully from Dureuil Janthial or Jacquesson; Givry from Joblot and Lumpp, to single out some who did especially well. They won’t disappoint, and they won’t be expensive.

William:

I haven’t seen your signature in WB before (I read here scantily) but am enjoying your balanced observations in this thread. Do you write exclusively for Decanter?

Apropos Chalonnaise, did you taste at de Villaine in '15-'16?

Cheers.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Ian Fitzsimmons:

William:

I haven’t seen your signature in WB before (I read here scantily) but am enjoying your balanced observations in this thread. Do you write exclusively for Decanter?

Apropos Chalonnaise, did you taste at de Villaine in '15-'16?

Cheers.

Thanks Ian. And yes, principally for Decanter for now.

I did taste de Villaine’s 2015s, and found them a touch more gourmand and textural than normal, but true to the domaine’s style and appellations. 2016s I missed, as Pierre was preoccupied with an exciting new project (which I imagine will be announced soon) during my two months in Burgundy, so I will have to wait until they are bottled to get hold of some samples.

Robert Panzer:

Disclaimer: I represent Amiot Servelle and Georges Noellat.

I think this is perfect.

I see why some are upset. No one wants Wine Talk to turn into a venue for retailers (or producers for that matter) pimping their latest wares. But if adequately disclosed, knowledgeable ITB’ers should feel free to weigh in on the merits of individual wines. I know that some other retailers/wholesalers will add the disclaimer at the bottom of a post that they sell (or represent) one of the wines in the post. That should be enough for people to make up their own minds as to how much, if any, stock to put in a note or a post.

posts.name: D@vid K@ntr0witz
posts.cooked:
Neal.Mollen:

But if adequately disclosed, knowledgeable ITB’ers should feel free to weigh in on the merits of individual wines. I know that some other retailers/wholesalers will add the disclaimer at the bottom of a post that they sell (or represent) one of the wines in the post. That should be enough for people to make up their own minds as to how much, if any, stock to put in a note or a post.

It’s better, but I think Robert’s disclaimer should be more along the lines of as follows:

“Disclaimer: I represent Amiot Servelle and Georges Noellat, posted an offer for their wines five minutes ago in Commerce Corner, never say anything bad about any vintage or producer I sell, and am of the opinion that most other retailers are unethical.”

I think that would be a slightly better fit.

That way, unwitting buyers like me will not be shammed into a purchase decision without full knowledge of all the intra-poster conflicts of interest. The nerve!
